title: generate systemd doesn't seem to be able to handle the container dependency issues brought by --volumes-from.
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.`podman run -d --name test1 -v ~/test:/var/test alpine`

2.`podman run -d --name test2 --volumes-from test1 alpine`

3.`podman generate systemd --new --files --name test1 && podman generate systemd --new --files --name test2`

**Describe the results you received:**
In fact, due to the dependency brought by --volumes-from, when the container test2 is started first, since the container test1 has not been started, it seems that the container test2 cannot be started normally.

**Describe the results you expected:**
Hopefully generate systemd will handle the dependencies brought by --volumes-from.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

```
cat container-test2.service
# container-test2.service
# autogenerated by Podman 3.4.4
# Sat Jan 29 18:36:44 CST 2022

[Unit]
Description=Podman container-test2.service
Documentation=man:podman-generate-systemd(1)
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target
RequiresMountsFor=%t/containers

[Service]
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
Restart=on-failure
TimeoutStopSec=70
ExecStartPre=/bin/rm -f %t/%n.ctr-id
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman run --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id --cgroups=no-conmon --rm --sdnotify=conmon --replace -d --name test2 --volumes-from test1 alpine
ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman stop --ignore --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
ExecStopPost=/usr/bin/podman rm -f --ignore --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
Type=notify
NotifyAccess=all

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

```

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

physical

comments.body: How should this work? You execute two different `podman generate systemd` commands. The second one cannot know how the unit for test1 is called or if it even exists so it cannot add a dependency.

@Luap99 Even if I configure the pod for the container and only generate the service file for the pod, the generate systemd still cannot handle the container dependency problem caused by --volumes-from.

Add the following dependencies to the `container-test2.service` file.
```
[Unit]
After=container-test1.service
```
This way both containers should work fine.

It should be possible with pods. I will label this as feature request.
I think it will be difficult to implement and I am not sure if it is worth it.

Note the comment for --new flag from the man page:

> The unit files are created best effort and may need to be further edited; please review the generated files
carefully before using them in production.
@vrothberg WDYT?

I am OK with supporting it but I don't think I will find time to implement it in the foreseeable future.  In a way, it's already supported but the user had to generate the units for each container and use the --dependency flag. Or, as Paul mentioned, by using Pods.

Something I want to work on after cosign is to support play-kube in generate systemd, which allows for running more complex workloads.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@flouthoc PTAL
@vrothberg something for interns?

> @vrothberg something for interns?

Not sure. I think it's best to use the `--after` flag.  The other containers have to be running in systemd units as well and we don't know the file names.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg Any other thoughts on this?`

* **container** - I think these dependencies must be resolved by the user.  Additional dependencies can be added via `podman generate systemd --after`.

* **pod** - https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13076#issuecomment-1027640337 suggests that volume-dependencies are not taken into account when generating units for a pod.  I did not test it myself but it should be possible.

My proposed tasks:
1. Create a reproducer for a pod with two containers where one container uses the volume(s) of the other
2. Fix the dependencies of the generated units if needed -> add dependencies to the `After=` field.
SGTM
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg Any time to work on this?
I probably won't find time

With the advent of [Quadlet ](https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/quadlet-podman), generate-systemd has been deprecated.  That means that no new features will be added to the command but only urgent bug fixes.  Hence, I am closing the issue as not planned.

With Quadlet `.volume` files this can be handled nicely.  Or via systemd-native dependency primitives such as After= or Requires=.
